Defendant The Lee Law Firm'S Motion For Order To Compel Binding Arbitration As To All Parties And Stay Action.
Off Calendar Jan-11-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
James Dombroski, Esq. 619-338-9500 for Plaintiff hrough Court Call
Sergio Stevens, Esq. 925-939-2000 of the Lee Hernandez Landrum Carlson for Defendant Lee Law Firm

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court denies Plaintiff's request to brief on a certain point. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

Defendant The Lee Law Firm'S Motion For Order To Compel Binding Arbitration As To All Parties And Stay Action.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.Defendants' (hereafter "Lee") motion to compel arbitration and stay is GRANTED.

The evidence establishes that the entities that are seeking arbitration are the same as the entity that entered into the arbitration agreement with plaintiffs. (Lee Reply Dec., par. 5.)

The motion to compel arbitration is timely and Rosenson v. Greenberg Glusker Fields Claman & Machtinger LLP (2012) 203 Cal.App.4th 688, 691 is inapposite. There, the court explains that the 30-day deadline applies to one seeking to avoid the Mandatory Fee Arbitration Act award. In this case, the award was in Lee's favor and Lee was not rejecting it. After plaintiffs rejected the award, "the action shall proceed in accordance with the provisions of Par 2 (commencing with section 307) of the Code of Civil Procedure, concerning civil actions generally." (Business & Professions Code 6204(c).)

The supplemental declaration of Sergio Stevens establishes that the Lee Law Firm is registered with the State Bar. (Stevens Dec., Ex. 3 and 4.) In addition, the failure of a firm to register with the State Bar in compliance with Business & Professions Code 6160 is not a ground to avoid arbitration. Such dereliction is ordinarily not the type of "inequitable" conduct or unclean hands/bad faith that compels denial a motion to compel arbitration. In Weisman v. Johnson (1982) 133 Cal.App.3d 289, the court denied arbitration because the party seeking arbitration engaged in delay tactics and made misstatements about his residence. The failure to properly register as a law corporation with the Bar has no effect on the recovery of fees. (See Olson v. Cohen (2003) 106 Cal.App.4th 1209.) "Incorporation as a professional corporation is not undertaken for the protection of clients." (Steven M. Garber & Associates v. Eskandarian (2007) 150 Cal.App.4th 813, 820.) Failing to register is a situation drastically different from practicing law in California without a license. There is no evidence that the members of the Lee Law Firm practiced law in California without a proper license. The court further notes that California has a "strong public policy in favor of arbitration." (Ahdout v. Hekmatjah (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 21, 33.)

Plaintiffs' argument that defendants violated CCP 1281.2 by failing to demand arbitration fails. That section provides that "[o]n petition of a party to an arbitration agreement alleging the existence of a written agreement to arbitrate a controversy and that a party thereto refuses to arbitrate such controversy'" In this case, defendants are proceeding by motion, not "petition." Plaintiffs' filing of this action showed its refusal to arbitrate.

Lastly, plaintiffs fail to show that the arbitration or retainer agreement is "illegal" such that it cannot be enforced. As noted, defendants have California bar licenses, were registered with the State Bar, and were not "out of state" attorneys illegally practicing law in California.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)
